grammarLessons.name: 은/는 vs 이/가 — Topic vs Subject

cards.grammarNote:
**안녕하세요** (annyeonghaseyo) is the standard polite greeting at any time of day.
**안녕** alone is casual — only use with close friends.
The **-세요** ending is an honorific imperative/polite form from **하다** (to do) — literally 'do peacefully'.
Formal: **안녕하십니까** (annyeonghasimnikka) — used in formal speeches or broadcasting.

**부탁하다** = to ask a favor / to request.
**부탁해요** at the end of a request softens it politely.
Alternative: **~주세요** (juseyo) — also very common: **커피 주세요** (coffee please).

**감사합니다** (gamsahamnida) is the formal thank-you — used with strangers, elders, or in professional settings.
**감사해요** (gamsahaeyo) is the polite but less formal version.
**고마워요** (gomawoyo) = casual thanks, fine with friends.
**감사** (感謝, gamsa) is Sino-Korean: **감** = feel + **사** = gratitude.
**-합니다/-ㅂ니다** is the formal speech ending, more formal than **-해요**.

Wanting a thing: **원해요** (wonhaeyo).
Wanting to do something: verb stem + **고 싶어요**: **먹고 싶어요** (I want to eat), **가고 싶어요** (I want to go).
**-고 싶어요** is one of the most useful endings to learn.

**필요하다** (piryohada) = to be necessary / to need.
**필요** (必要, piryo) is Sino-Korean: **필** = must + **요** = need — same characters as Japanese 必要 (hitsuyō).
Structure: [thing needed] + **이/가 필요해요** — the needed item takes the subject particle **이/가**, not the object particle.
**도움이 필요해요** = I need help (lit. 'help is needed').

**이에요** after a consonant, **예요** after a vowel: **제 이름은 Ana예요** (Ana ends in a vowel).
Formal: **제 이름은 ~입니다**.
Casual: **나 ~야**.

**저** = formal/polite 'I' (with strangers or elders).
**나** = casual 'I' (with friends).
**저는** pairs with **해요**-style verbs; **나는** pairs with casual speech.

**당신** = formal 'you', but often avoided — sounds cold between strangers, awkward between friends.
Koreans usually use the person's name or title instead.
**너** = casual 'you' with close friends only.

**그** = he (also 'that' as a demonstrative).
**그녀** (geunyeo) = she — but both pronouns are rare in natural speech.
Koreans typically use the person's name, a title, or just drop the subject entirely when context is clear.
**그는** (topic) = as for him; **그가** (subject) = he [as subject].

**그녀** (geunyeo) = she — a literary/written form, rarely used in spoken Korean.
In speech, Koreans use the person's name, a title (**언니**, **누나**, **선생님**), or simply drop the pronoun.
**여기서** = 'here at / from here' — the particle **서** attached to a location word marks where an action takes place.

**주무세요** is the honorific form of **자다** (to sleep) — always use it with elders or anyone you show respect to.
**잘 자요** (jal jayo) = casual 'sleep well' — use with friends.
**안녕히** (annyeonghi) = peacefully — the same root as **안녕하세요**.

**잘 지내요?** = How are you? (polite, casual)
**잘** = well, **지내다** = to get along / to be doing.
Common response: **잘 지내요** (I'm doing well).
cards.englishPhrase: How are you? I'm doing well, thanks.
cards.phrase: 잘 지내요? 네, 잘 지내요, 감사해요.
cards.phraseRomanized: Jal jinaeyo? Ne, jal jinaeyo, gamsahaeyo.

cards.grammarNote:
**처음 뵙겠습니다** = nice to meet you (very formal).
**반갑습니다** (bangapseumnida) = nice to meet you / glad to meet you — also common.
**처음** = first time, **뵙다** = humble form of 'to see/meet'.
cards.englishPhrase: Nice to meet you. I'm Jeff.

cards.grammarNote:
**천만에요** (cheonmaneyo) = 'not at all / don't mention it' — literally 'ten million [no]'.
More common in modern speech: **아니에요** (anieyo) = 'it's nothing'.
**별말씀을요** (byeolmalsseumeulyo) = 'not at all / it was nothing' — more natural and humble.

**그것** = that thing / it.
In speech: **그게** (subject form), **그걸** (object form).
Koreans often drop the pronoun entirely when context is clear.

**우리** = we, but also used where English says 'my': **우리 집** (my house), **우리 엄마** (my mom).
This 'our' for 'my' reflects a cultural sense of shared belonging — very natural in Korean.

**그들** (geudeul) = they — **들** is the Korean plural suffix.
**들** can be added to many nouns: **학생들** (students), **친구들** (friends).
Korean doesn't require plural marking — it's optional and context often makes it clear.
Like **그/그녀**, **그들** is more literary; in conversation, Koreans name people directly.

**이것** = this thing (stands alone); **이** = this + noun: **이 책** (this book).
Same distance pattern: **그것/그** (that, near listener), **저것/저** (that over there, far from both).

Korean has a three-way demonstrative system:
**이** = this (near speaker), **그** = that (near listener), **저** = that (far from both).
**저 사람** = that person over there — notice the phrase uses **저**, not **그**.
In the example sentence, **저** is used as a demonstrative adjective before **사람**, not as the pronoun 'I'.

**제** (je) = polite/formal 'my' — a contracted form of **저의** (jeoui).
**내** (nae) = casual 'my' — contracted from **나의** (naui).
Use **제** with strangers and elders; **내** with friends.
**이름은** — the topic particle **은/는** is used here because the name is being presented/introduced, setting it up as the topic.

**누구** (nugu) = who.
With the subject particle it becomes **누가** (nuga), not **누구가**: **누가 왔어요?** (who came?).
With the topic particle: **누구는** — rarely used; context usually makes it clear.
**저 사람은 누구예요?** — note the topic particle **은** on **사람**, framing 'that person' as the topic being identified.

**뭐** (mwo) = what — the colloquial spoken form of **무엇** (mueot).
With the subject particle: **뭐가** (mwoga) — e.g., **뭐가 좋아요?** (what's good?).
With the object particle: **뭘** (mwol) — contraction of **뭐를**: **뭘 먹어요?** (what are you eating?).
In casual speech, **뭐** often stands alone without a particle.

**언제** (eonje) = when — used both as a question word and in embedded clauses.
**언제 와요?** = When are you coming? (question)
**언제 오는지 몰라요** = I don't know when they're coming (embedded clause).
Time question words: **언제** (when), **몇 시** (what time), **며칠** (what day of the month).

**어디** (eodi) = where.
With **예요/이에요** it means 'where is': **화장실이 어디예요?** (where is the bathroom?)
**어디서** = where (at/from) — marks location of an action: **어디서 왔어요?** (where are you from?)
**화장실** (化粧室, hwajangsil) is Sino-Korean: **화장** = makeup/adornment + **실** = room.

**왜** (wae) = why.
**못** (mot) = cannot (inability) — different from **안** (an) which means 'not' (choice/habit).
**왜 못 와요?** = Why can't you come? (inability — e.g., stuck at work)
**왜 안 와요?** = Why aren't you coming? (choice — suggests they could but aren't).
The **못** vs **안** distinction is important in Korean negation.

**어떻게** (eotteoke) = how — the adverb form of **어떻다** (eotteota, to be how/like what).
**어떻게 지내요?** = How are you? (literally 'how are you passing time?')
**어때요?** (eottaeyo) = 'how about it? / how is it?' — used to propose things or ask opinions.
**어떻게** describes the manner of an action; **어때요** asks for a judgment or preference.

**안녕하세요** works at any time of day — the most natural all-purpose greeting.
**좋은 아침이에요** is understood but less common in daily speech.
**안녕히 주무셨어요?** = 'did you sleep well?' (honorific morning greeting).
cards.englishPhrase: Good morning! Did you sleep well?

cards.grammarNote:
**찾다** (chatda) = to look for / to find.
**-고 있어요** (-go isseoyo) forms the present progressive: **찾고 있어요** = 'am looking for' (actively searching right now).
**출구** (出口, chulgu) is Sino-Korean: **출** = exit + **구** = opening/door — same characters as Japanese 出口 (deguchi).
The object particle **를** (reul) follows **출구** because it ends in a vowel.

**저기요** = 'excuse me!' for getting a stranger's or server's attention — very common in everyday speech.
**실례합니다** = 'excuse me' in more formal contexts (e.g., passing someone in a hallway).
cards.englishPhrase: Excuse me, where is the station?

cards.grammarNote:
verb stem + **(으)ㄹ 수 있다** = can/be able to.
**갈 수 있어요** (can go), **먹을 수 있어요** (can eat).
Negative: **(으)ㄹ 수 없다** = can't.
One of the most important patterns in Korean.

**모르다** (moreuda) = to not know — an irregular verb: the **르** becomes **ㄹㄹ** before vowels: **몰라요** (present), **몰랐어요** (past).
**어디 있는지 몰라요** = I don't know where it is — **-는지** is the indirect question ending, turning the question ('where is it?') into an embedded clause.
**알아요** (arayo) = I know; **몰라요** = I don't know — opposites.
cards.englishPhrase: I don't know where it is.

cards.grammarNote:
**있다** handles both 'to have' and 'to exist/there is'.
**돈이 있어요** = I have money (lit. 'money exists').
Opposite: **없다** (eopda) = don't have / doesn't exist.
No separate 'have' verb is needed.
cards.englishPhrase: I'm hungry. Do you have water?

cards.grammarNote:
**하다** (hada) = to do — the most versatile verb in Korean.
Many Sino-Korean nouns become verbs simply by adding **하다**: **공부하다** (to study), **전화하다** (to call), **요리하다** (to cook).
**뭐 해요?** = What are you doing? — **해요** is the polite present of **하다**.
**뭐 했어요?** = What did you do? — **했어요** is the past tense.

**먹다** (meokda) = to eat (neutral/informal register).
**드시다** (deusida) = the honorific form — use with elders: **드세요** (please eat).
**먹어요** = polite present; **먹었어요** = polite past.
**어디서** = 'where (at)' — the **서** particle on a location word marks where an action is performed, not just where something exists.

**좋아하다** (joahada) = to like (an active feeling toward something).
**좋다** (jota) = to be good / to like (a state — the thing IS good to you).
**커피를 좋아해요** = I like coffee (**좋아하다**, active).
**커피가 좋아요** = Coffee is good / I like coffee (**좋다**, state).
Both mean roughly the same in English, but structurally they differ.

**하지만** (hajiman) = but — a standalone conjunction between sentences.
**-지만** can also attach directly to a verb stem: **가고 싶지만** = 'I want to go, but...'.
**그런데** (geureonde) = 'but / however / by the way' — softer contrast or topic shift.
**못** before a verb = cannot: **못 가요** = can't go.
cards.englishPhrase: I want to go, but I can't.

cards.grammarNote:
**그리고** = 'and' between sentences.
**와/과** = 'and' between nouns: **와** after a vowel, **과** after a consonant.
Casual alternative: **이랑/랑** — more colloquial.

**아니면** (animyeon) = or — literally 'if not'.
**또는** (ttoneun) = or — slightly more formal/written.
**차 아니면 커피?** = tea or coffee? — the **아니면** sits between the two options, just like English 'or'.
**아니** = no/not — so **아니면** literally means 'if [it is] not [the first thing], then...'.

**배고프다** = to be hungry (**배** = stomach/belly) → **배고파요** in speech.
Compare: **배부르다** = to be full.
Korean uses its own adjective — not a 'have hunger' construction like Spanish or French.

**목이 마르다** = literally 'throat is dry' → to be thirsty.
**목** = throat/neck; **마르다** = to be dry/to dry out.
The subject particle **이** follows **목** because it ends in a consonant.
Compare: **배고프다** (hungry) also builds hunger as a body-state, not a possession.

**사람** (saram) = person (native Korean word).
**분** (bun) = person (honorific counter) — use when referring to someone you respect: **한 분** (one person [honorific]).
**명** (myeong) = person (counter for people in general): **두 명** (two people).
**저 사람** = that person over there — **저** here is the far demonstrative, not the pronoun 'I'.

**장소** (場所, jangso) is Sino-Korean: **장** = place + **소** = location — same characters as Japanese 場所 (basho).
**곳** (got) = place (native Korean) — used more naturally in everyday speech: **좋은 곳** (a good place).
**알아요?** = do you know? — **알다** is an irregular ㄹ verb; the **ㄹ** drops before **아요**: **알아요**, not **알라요**.
cards.englishPhrase: Do you know a good place?

cards.grammarNote:
**것** (geot) = thing — extremely common in Korean grammar.
In speech it contracts: **이것 → 이게** (this[subj]), **그것 → 그게** (that[subj]).
**것** also nominalizes verbs: **먹는 것** (the thing of eating = eating), **좋은 것** (a good thing).
In casual speech, **거** is used instead of **것**: **좋은 거예요** (it's a good thing).

**여기** (yeogi) = here (near speaker).
**거기** (geogi) = there (near listener).
**저기** (jeogi) = over there (far from both).
**여기 와요!** = Come here! — **와요** is the polite present of **오다** (to come); it works as both a statement and a mild imperative from context.

**거기** (geogi) = there (near the listener); **저기** (jeogi) = over there (far from both).
In the example, **저기예요** uses **저기** (over there) — the bathroom is far from both speakers.
**화장실은** — the topic particle **은** on **화장실** frames it as something already established in the conversation: 'as for the bathroom'.

**너무** = originally 'too much', but used colloquially as 'very/so': **너무 좋아요** (so good!).
**매우** = the more formal 'very'.
**정말** = 'really/truly' — very common in speech.

Two goodbye forms depending on who is leaving:
**안녕히 가세요** — said to the person leaving ('go peacefully').
**안녕히 계세요** — said by the person leaving to whoever stays ('stay peacefully').
Casual: **잘 가** / **잘 있어**.

**물** (mul) = water — a native Korean word.
**한 잔** = one glass/cup — **잔** is the counter for cups of liquid. Native Korean numbers are used: **한** (one), **두** (two), **세** (three).
**주세요** (juseyo) = please give me — the standard polite request at restaurants and cafés.

**음식** (飮食, eumsik) is Sino-Korean: **음** = drink + **식** = food — same characters as Japanese 飲食 (inshoku).
**맛있어요** (masisseoyo) = delicious — from **맛** (taste) + **있다** (to have/exist): 'to have taste'.
**음식이** — the subject particle **이** follows because **음식** ends in a consonant **ㄱ**.

**집** (jib) = house/home — native Korean.
**우리 집** = my house — Koreans say 'our house' (**우리**) even when meaning 'my house', reflecting a collective cultural identity.
**가까워요** is the polite present of **가깝다** (to be close/near) — a **ㅂ** irregular adjective: **ㅂ** changes to **우** before a vowel ending.

**가게** (gage) = store/shop (general).
**상점** (商店, sangjeom) = store (more formal/Sino-Korean).
**가게가** — the subject particle **가** follows because **가게** ends in a vowel.
**닫았어요** = closed (past tense) — **닫다** (to close) + **았어요** (past polite). Compare: **열었어요** = opened.

**현금** (現金, hyeongeum) = cash — Sino-Korean: **현** = present/current + **금** = gold/money.
**카드** (kadeu) = card — an English loanword (phonetic borrowing).
**현금이에요, 카드예요?** = Cash or card? — notice **이에요** after **현금** (ends in consonant **ㅁ**) and **예요** after **카드** (ends in vowel).
Korea is a highly digital society — most transactions are by card or phone payment.

**도움** (doum) = help (noun).
**돕다** (dopda) = to help (verb) — a **ㅂ** irregular: **도와요** (helps), **도와줘요** (please help).
**도움이 필요해요** = I need help — literally 'help is needed'. The needed thing takes **이/가**, not the object particle.
Common phrase: **도와주세요** (dowajuseyo) = please help me.

**화장실** (化粧室, hwajangsil) = bathroom/restroom — Sino-Korean: **화장** = makeup/adornment + **실** = room.
In Korea, **화장실** refers to the toilet room (not a room with a bath); asking for it is perfectly natural.
**공중화장실** (gongjung hwajangsil) = public restroom.
**화장실이** — subject particle **이** after consonant **ㄹ**.

**항상** (hangsang) = always — a Sino-Korean adverb.
**언제나** (eonjena) = always (native Korean equivalent, slightly softer).
**아침에** = in the morning — the particle **에** marks a time point.
**커피를** — object particle **를** after **커피** (ends in vowel).
**마셔요** = polite present of **마시다** (to drink).
cards.englishPhrase: I always drink coffee in the morning.

cards.grammarNote:
**절대로** = absolutely/never — must be paired with a negative verb: **절대로 안 가요** (I never go).
**한 번도 ~않다** = not even once.
The negative marker **안** or **~지 않다** is required.

**가다** (gada) = to go — one of the most fundamental Korean verbs.
**가게에** = to the store — the particle **에** marks the destination.
**가게에 가다** = go to the store (note: **에** is repeated because **가게** itself ends in **에** coincidentally — they are different **에**).
**오다** (oda) = to come — the opposite of **가다**.

**오다** (oda) = to come — the opposite of **가다** (to go).
**왔어요** = came (past polite) — **오** + **았어요** contracts to **왔어요**.
**어디서** = from where — **서** attached to a location marks origin or the place of action.
**어디서 왔어요?** = Where are you from? (lit. 'from where did you come?') — a very common question when meeting someone.

**이해하다** (理解, ihaehada) = to understand — Sino-Korean: **이** = reason + **해** = untangle/explain.
**이해 못 해요** = I can't understand — **못** before **하다** expresses inability.
Alternative: **이해가 안 돼요** (I don't understand — lit. 'understanding doesn't become').
Also common: **모르겠어요** (moreugesseoyo) = I don't know / I can't figure it out.

**말하다** (malhada) = to speak/talk.
**말** (mal) = word/speech — native Korean.
**한국어** = Korean language — **한국** (Korea) + **어** (language); similarly **영어** (English), **일본어** (Japanese), **중국어** (Chinese).
**한국어 말해요?** = Do you speak Korean? — no subject particle on **한국어** in casual speech; in formal speech: **한국어를 하세요?**

**주다** = to give.
**~아/어 주세요** = please do [something] for me: **도와주세요** (please help me), **가르쳐 주세요** (please teach me).
**~아/어 주다** frames actions as favors — very common in polite requests.

**보다** (boda) = to see / to look at / to watch.
**아무것도** = anything / nothing at all — **아무것** (anything) + **도** (also/even) in a negative sentence means 'nothing at all'.
**못** before a verb = cannot (inability).
**아무것도 못 봐요** = I can't see anything (lit. 'even anything cannot see').
**봐요** = polite present of **보다**; the **오** vowel contracts with **아**: **보 + 아요 → 봐요**.

**와/과** (wa/gwa) = and/with — **와** after a vowel, **과** after a consonant.
**함께** (hamkke) = together — slightly formal; **같이** (gachi) is the casual equivalent.
**친구와 함께 왔어요** = came with a friend.
**왔어요** = came (past polite) — **오다** + **았어요** → **왔어요** (irregular contraction).

**없이** (eopsi) = without — derived from **없다** (to not have/not exist) + the adverb-forming suffix **이**.
**설탕 없이** = without sugar — the noun comes directly before **없이**.
Contrast: **설탕이 없어요** = there is no sugar (statement); **설탕 없이** = without sugar (adverbial modifier).
**주세요** = please give me — the standard polite ordering phrase.

**위해** (wihae) / **위한** (wihan) = for (the benefit/sake of).
**당신을 위한 거예요** = 'it's a thing for you' — **위한** modifies a following noun (**거/것**).
**이건** = **이것은** contracted — 'this thing [topic]'.
**거예요** = **것이에요** contracted — 'is a thing'.
**을 위해** takes the object particle **을/를** before it.

**에** = direction or static location: **학교에 가요** (go to school), **집에 있어요** (at home).
**에서** = where an action takes place: **카페에서 먹어요** (eat at the café).
Rule: use **에** for 'where something is', **에서** for 'where you do something'.
cards.englishPhrase: The book is on the table.

cards.grammarNote:
**에서** (eseo) serves double duty: it marks the place of an action AND origin/source.
**어디서 왔어요?** = Where are you from? (**에서** = from).
**카페에서 먹어요** = eating at the café (**에서** = location of action).
Context makes the meaning clear — both uses share the same particle.

**좋다** (jota) = to be good — a Korean adjective verb (acts as both adjective and predicate).
**좋은** (joeun) = good (modifier form before a noun): **좋은 생각** (good idea).
**좋아요** = it's good (predicate form).
**생각** (saenggak) = thought/idea — native Korean.
**이에요** after **생각** (ends in consonant **ㄱ**) — not **예요**.

**나쁘다** (nappeuda) = to be bad — an adjective verb.
**나빠요** = polite present — **나쁘** + **아요**: the **으** drops before a vowel ending.
**그건** = **그것은** contracted — 'that thing [topic]'.
**나쁜** = bad (modifier before a noun): **나쁜 습관** (bad habit).
Opposite: **좋다** (good) → **좋아요**.

**크다** (keuda) = to be big/large — an adjective verb.
**큰** (keun) = big (modifier form): **큰 커피** (a large coffee).
**커요** = it's big (predicate, present polite) — **크** + **어요**: the **으** drops.
**커피** (keopi) = coffee — an English loanword. Many food and drink words in Korean come from English or other Western languages.

**작다** (jakda) = to be small — an adjective verb.
**작은** (jageun) = small (modifier before a noun): **작은 테이블** (a small table).
**작아요** = it's small (predicate, present polite).
**테이블** (teibeul) = table — an English loanword. Korean uses the honorific form with customers: **주세요** (please give/provide).

**많이** (mani) = a lot, much — the adverb form of the adjective **많다** (manta, to be many/much).
**많아요** = there is a lot (predicate form).
**할 일이 많아요** = I have a lot of work to do — literally 'work-to-do is many'.
**할 일** = work to do — **할** is the modifier form of **하다** (to do) on the noun **일** (work): 'work [that one] will do'.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of work.

cards.grammarNote:
**조금** (jogeum) = a little / a small amount — used both as an adverb and before nouns.
**좀** (jom) = a slightly more casual, shorter form of **조금** — also common as a softener in requests: **좀 도와주세요** (could you help me a little?).
**물 조금 주세요** = please give me a little water — **주세요** without an object particle is natural in informal ordering.

**어떻게** = how (adverb of manner).
**한국어로** = in Korean — **로** after **어** (vowel) marks the language or means: **영어로** (in English), **한국어로** (in Korean).
**말해요** = present polite of **말하다** (to say/speak).
The topic particle **는** is used on the foreign phrase being asked about — it sets it up as the subject of inquiry.
cards.englishPhrase: How do you say 'thank you' in Korean?

cards.grammarNote:
**도와줄 수 있어요?** = Can you help me? — literally 'do you have the ability to help-give?'
**도와** = stem of **돕다** (to help) after the **ㅂ** → **우** irregular change.
**줄** = modifier form of **주다** (to give/do for).
**수 있다** = can/be able to — the key ability pattern: verb stem + **(으)ㄹ 수 있어요**.
The **주다** adds the nuance of 'doing something as a favor for me'.

**얼마예요?** (eolmayeyo) = How much is it? — the essential shopping phrase.
**얼마** = how much — a question word for price or quantity.
**예요** after **얼마** (ends in vowel): **이에요** after a consonant, **예요** after a vowel.
Alternative: **얼마입니까?** (formal); **얼마야?** (very casual).
Korean prices use Sino-Korean numbers: **천 원** (1,000 won), **만 원** (10,000 won).

**죄송해요** = formal apology (with strangers or elders).
**미안해요** = casual sorry (with friends).
**미안해** = very casual.
Koreans apologize frequently as a sign of social consideration.

**괜찮아요** (gwaenchanayo) = it's okay / no problem / are you alright?
**괜찮다** functions as both 'it's fine' (reassurance) and 'are you okay?' (concern) — context determines the meaning.
**걱정 마세요** = don't worry — **걱정** (worry, noun) + **마세요** (please don't, negative polite imperative from **말다**).
**마세요** = please don't [do something] — a very useful polite negative command.
